public.title: Jorgen and His Times - Passing down the family story
public.summary: I turned my great-great-grandfather's typewritten immigration story into a printed book as a Christmas gift for my dad—and it became so meaningful, we ended up making 11 copies for the whole family.
public.description:
A year or two ago, my dad handed me a stack of papers clipped together. His grandma (my great-grandma) had written a story about her father immigrating to Canada from Denmark. I'd never known it existed.

He mentioned he should probably read it again as he hadn't read it in decades, and that this was the only copy he knew about. That's when I got the idea: I wanted to turn it into a real book for him.

Fast forward to Christmas 2025. I finally decided to make it happen.

## The Plan

I needed to:
1. Get the pages scanned
2. Reformat the typewritten text into a digital version
3. Figure out how to get it printed and bound

Since I wasn't living with my parents anymore, I asked my mom to find the stack of papers and scan them. She works at a school and was able to handle the scanning of all 100-plus pages of it.

## The Big Challenge: OCR Hell

Once I had the PDF, I ran it through an OCR tool to convert the scanned images into editable text. That's where things got messy.

The pages had been typed on a typewriter and had picked up dirt and wear from being photocopied. When OCR processed them, it introduced tons of errors: missing characters, extra characters, punctuation mistakes, formatting issues everywhere.

There was no shortcut. I had to read through the entire book line by line, correcting every single error. It took me two to three weeks, but once I was done, I finally had a clean, digital manuscript.

I also made sure to include some handwritten notes my grandma had left for my dad, my uncle, and other family members.

## Finding a Printer

There was a custom bookbinder near my parents' house. I stopped in and asked about pricing. A single professionally bound copy would cost around $250.

That was expensive, and I wanted to make more than one copy. So I started looking for alternatives.

I found Lulu, an online printing service specializing in custom books. The process was straightforward: design a cover, upload the PDF, pay, and order. I pulled my graphic design background to create a nice little cover for the book too, adding my personal touch to the family heirloom.

It was also affordable enough that I could order multiple copies.

## The Payoff

The books arrived just in time for Christmas. I gave copies to my dad and my uncle at Christmas dinner.

They absolutely loved it.

In fact, they loved it so much that we ended up ordering another nine copies to share with the rest of the family.

It was incredibly rewarding to put in that time and effort for something so meaningful. Something that can be reprinted and shared as our family grows. Reading Jorgen's story gave me a deeper appreciation for the hardships my great-great-grandfather and his family endured when immigrating to Canada. It's not a happy story, but it's an inspiring one.

Learning about my family's history was humbling in the best way possible.

I've included a link below to the PDF version of Jorgen and His Times if you'd like to read it for yourself.

portfolio.title: Jorgen and His Times: Family Memoir Publishing
portfolio.summary: Digitized, edited, and published a 100+ page family memoir as a custom printed book, managing OCR correction, graphic design, and print production.
portfolio.description:
## Problem
My dad had a single copy of a typewritten story written by his grandmother about her father's immigration to Canada from Denmark. It was fragile, at risk of being lost, and only existed in analog form. I wanted to preserve it and create a meaningful gift.

## Approach
I developed a multi-phase plan: (1) Scanned 100+ typewritten pages into a digital PDF, (2) Manually corrected extensive OCR errors line-by-line over 2-3 weeks to create a clean manuscript, (3) Designed a custom book cover using graphic design skills, (4) Researched printing options and selected an online service (Lulu) for affordability and quality, (5) Managed timeline to deliver by Christmas.

## Outcome
Successfully produced initial copies for my dad and uncle by Christmas. The positive reception led to printing 11 total copies for extended family distribution. Created a lasting, shareable family artifact that preserves generational history and enables future reprinting as the family grows.

public.title: scout - A Personal Job Search Companion
public.summary: A locally hosted job search dashboard that uses AI to analyze job postings against a personal candidate profile, returning fit scores, structured breakdowns, and ready-to-use application materials.
public.description:
I built a tool to make job searching less exhausting. Here is why I am sharing it.

I am in the middle of a job search that involves a career change, and like a lot of people in that position, I found that most postings take real effort to evaluate properly. A title can look like a strong match and then bury a requirement several paragraphs down that rules it out entirely: years of specific industry experience, a certification, or a salary below what is workable.

Reading every listing closely, across multiple job boards, several times a week, adds up to a lot of repetitive work. So I built a small dashboard called Scout that reads postings and gives me a structured, honest read on whether each one is worth my time.
What it does

You paste in the text of a job posting from anywhere, a job board, a company careers page, or an email alert, and within about thirty seconds, you get back the following. 

- A fit score out of 10 with a plain language explanation of why, written specifically against your background and experience.
- Salary, seniority level, and industry are pulled directly from the posting and displayed in your preferred currency.
- Whether the role is remote, hybrid, or on-site, provide the work address, a Google Maps link, and an estimated driving distance from your location.
- Benefits and compensation details are called out separately so they are easy to compare.
- A public reputation summary for the employer, including a rating, pros, cons, and a source.
- Resume and cover letter keywords, sorted into hard skills, soft skills, and industry terms, each clickable to copy.

You can save promising roles, star the ones that stand out as priorities, and mark the ones you have applied to. Each tracked application carries fields for the date applied, a follow-up checkbox with a date, a contact name, email, LinkedIn profile link, a direct link back to the posting, and a notes section. There is also a built-in contact search that tries to find a publicly listed HR or hiring manager email for the company.

Everything is stored privately on your own device. Nothing is sent to a shared server or outside account.


## Why is the scoring strict on purpose?

Most tools like this default to being encouraging. This one does not. If a posting wants several years of experience in a field you have not worked in, it gets scored low, clearly and specifically, not softened. That is the entire point. A tool that only tells you what you want to hear adds noise to a process that already has too much of it. The scoring is designed to give an honest second opinion rather than a flattering one.


## Who is this for?

- Anyone applying to a meaningful volume of postings and trying to sort through them quickly.
- Career changers, where it can be genuinely hard to tell from a title alone whether your background transfers.
- Anyone who wants resume and cover letter keywords pulled out automatically instead of doing it by hand for every application. 
- Anyone who wants a private, offline system for tracking applications without creating another account on another platform.


## Where this is headed

Right now, this is a personal tool I use daily. I am exploring turning it into something others can access directly through a dedicated website. Pricing is still being worked out, and the goal is to keep it as accessible as possible for people who are actively job searching. If that sounds useful, keep an eye on gavinmakesstuff.com for updates, or reach out through the contact page.

*I did not build this to launch a product on day one. I built it because I needed it, and sharing it felt like the natural next step.*

portfolio.title: scout - A Personal Job Search Companion
portfolio.summary: A locally hosted job search dashboard that uses AI to analyze job postings against a personal candidate profile, returning fit scores, structured breakdowns, and ready-to-use application materials.
portfolio.description:
##Project Overview

Scout is a self-built, locally hosted job search dashboard that uses artificial intelligence to analyze job postings against a personal candidate profile. Rather than manually reading and evaluating every posting found while job hunting, the tool accepts any pasted job description and returns a complete, structured breakdown: a numeric fit score out of ten, a plain language explanation of that score, required qualifications, compensation and benefits, public employer reputation data, commute distance, work location type, and ready-to-use resume and cover letter keywords.

I designed and continue to build this application independently, taking an iterative approach to development: describing what I need in plain language and refining the result through many rounds of testing and real use. The project has grown steadily alongside my own active job search, with new features added in direct response to problems I actually encountered.

Ease of use and convenience were the primary design goals throughout. Every decision, from the single paste-and-click workflow to the automatic keyword extraction and one-click application tracking, was made to remove friction from a process that is naturally repetitive and draining. 


##The Problem

I am in an active job search targeting a career change, bringing a mid-level technology background and a recently earned professional certification. This kind of pivot search carries a specific challenge: postings can look promising on the surface but actually require years of direct industry experience or professional designations I do not yet hold. Reading every posting in full to find that out is slow, and it is easy to either waste time on poor fit roles or second guess genuinely strong ones.

I needed a tool that could do three things consistently: tell me quickly whether a posting is actually worth pursuing, surface all the practical details such as pay, location, benefits, commute distance, and employer reputation without digging through the full listing, and help me tailor application materials faster and more accurately.


## The Solution

- Scout addresses this directly. The workflow is simple by design.
Paste the full text of one or more job postings into the dashboard. Multiple postings can be queued and analyzed together in a single pass.
- The system analyzes each posting against a stored candidate profile covering background, target industry, minimum salary and currency, years of experience, certifications, and stated career goals.
- Each posting returns a 1-to-10 fit score with a specific written rationale, not just a vague label.
- Supporting details are surfaced automatically: salary, seniority level, required qualifications, benefits, employer reputation, and whether the role is remote, hybrid, or on-site, including an estimated commute distance and a Google Maps link. 
- Resume and cover letter keywords are extracted in the same pass and organized into hard skills, soft skills, and industry terminology. Promising roles can be bookmarked, starred for priority, or marked as applied, building a running, searchable record of the search with application dates, follow-up tracking, contact details, notes, and direct links to each posting.

## Key Design Goals

- Ease of use first.
- The entire workflow is built around a single paste-and-click action. There are no setup steps between finding a posting and getting a clear answer on it.
- Genuine convenience.
- Details that would normally take several minutes to find across multiple tabs, such as benefits, employer reputation, and commute distance, are gathered automatically into one structured card.
- Honest assessment over encouragement. 
- The scoring logic is designed to give a candid read rather than a flattering one. A tool that tells you what you want to hear adds noise rather than clarity.
- A living tool, not a finished product.
- Scout continues to evolve alongside my actual job search, with features added in direct response to real needs as they come up.

## Full Feature Set

**Strict 1-to-10 fit scoring with written rationale.**
Scoring logic explicitly penalizes postings that require years of industry experience the candidate does not have, professional designations they do not hold, or salary below their stated minimum.

**Resume-based profile building. **
Uploading a PDF or Word resume automatically extracts and populates the full candidate profile, including inferred salary expectations and currency.

**Multi-currency support. **
The profile stores a preferred currency. Salary expectations are passed to the analysis in that currency, and postings in a different currency are returned with the original currency noted clearly rather than converted.

**Multi-posting batch analysis. **
Several postings can be pasted into separate numbered slots and analyzed together in a single API call.

**Work location and commute. **
Each card shows whether a role is remote, hybrid, or on site, with the work address, a Google Maps link, and an estimated driving distance from the user's saved location.

**Employer reputation context. **
Each result includes a public sentiment summary with a rating, pros, cons, and a cited source, so salary and benefits are not evaluated in isolation from workplace quality.

**Keyword extraction. **
Every analysis returns categorized keyword sets ready to drop into resume and cover letter tailoring. Individual keywords copy on click. 

**Star highlighting.**
Any posting can be starred to mark it as a priority. Starring an unsaved posting saves it automatically so the highlight persists.

**Application tracking. **
Saved and applied postings carry fields for date applied, follow-up sent checkbox with a date, notes, contact name, contact email, HR contact LinkedIn profile, and a manually editable posting URL.

**Contact finder. **
A dedicated search function attempts to surface a publicly available HR or hiring manager contact for each company, with a fallback for manual entry.

**Collapsible cards. **
Each card can be collapsed to just its header and score for faster scanning across a long results list. 

**Mini preview strips. **
The Saved and Applied tabs show a scrollable strip of quick preview cards at the top so the user can see what is in each list at a glance without fully switching tabs. 

**First visit onboarding.**
A welcome prompt appears exactly once on the first-ever use, inviting the user to set up their profile before running their first analysis. 

**In-app usage guide.**
A collapsible step-by-step guide lives just below the nav bar, covering all eight key workflows.

**Dark and light themes.**
A theme toggle in the nav bar switches between a clean light mode and a deep navy dark mode, with the choice persisted across sessions.


## Technical Approach

The application runs client-side entirely as a static HTML, CSS, and JavaScript project with a clean file structure separating markup, styling, and logic across five files for maintainability. It calls a large language model API directly from the browser to perform job analysis, resume parsing, keyword extraction, and contact searching, with structured prompts engineered to enforce consistent, strict scoring.

All personal data, including the candidate profile, saved postings, applied jobs, location, and theme preference, is stored locally in the browser. No account creation, external database, or server is required to use the application.

The application uses the Scout brand identity, a custom teal and sand palette with Nunito typography, a responsive card-based layout, and a branded logo throughout.


## Challenges and Solutions

**Job boards such as LinkedIn block automated page access.**
Shifted from a URL fetch model to a paste-based workflow, which works with any source, including job boards, company career pages, and email alerts, with no blocked access.

**Early scoring was too generous and rated clearly unqualified roles as viable.**
Rebuilt the scoring instructions with explicit cap rules. Postings requiring years of missing industry experience or professional designations that the candidate does not hold are scored low by rule, not by inference. 

**Browser data was being unexpectedly cleared between sessions.**
Identified an aggressive privacy setting in the Brave browser and adjusted site data storage permissions so saved postings and profile data persist reliably.

**The profile modal was too narrow for comfortable editing. **
Widened the modal to 960px with a two-column field grid so longer entries are easy to read and edit.

**No visual feedback during a 20-40-second analysis call.**
Added a staged progress indicator with descriptive step labels that update through the analysis, ending with a green completion state.

**Salary and fit scoring did not account for the user's preferred currency.**
Added a currency selector to the profile. The prompt now passes the user's currency context and instructs the model to keep salary figures in the original posting currency rather than converting.

## Ongoing Use and Direction
Scout is an active part of my day-to-day job search. Every posting I consider goes through it first. It has shortened the time spent evaluating individual listings, given me a consistent basis for comparing very different roles, and produced ready-to-use keyword sets that feed directly into tailoring my resume and cover letters for each application.

Beyond the practical benefit, the project has become a demonstrable skill: proof that I can scope a real problem, design a working solution, and steadily refine it through genuine use. I am currently exploring turning Scout into a standalone website that others can access, with a pricing model that keeps it as affordable as possible while remaining sustainable to run.
